roots.title: Gradient Descent Overview and Midterm Details
roots.summary: The lecture covers an overview of gradient descent, including stochastic gradient descent and its significance in AI applications. Midterm exam details are provided: it is scheduled for Wednesday, October 23rd, from 7 to 9 PM, with a review session on Friday in Evans 10 from 1:30 to 3:00 PM, available for 230 students on a first-come, first-serve basis. Recordings and slides will be accessible on Ed after the session, and seating assignments will be given on Monday, October 21st. Students requiring accommodations must complete a specific form. The gradient descent section includes optimization techniques, the model and loss function using mean squared error (MSE), iterative update formulas for model parameters, and the importance of initial guesses. It discusses gradient descent in one-dimensional and multi-dimensional contexts, covering batch, mini-batch, and stochastic gradient descent, with visual representations of the MSE curve. The update rules for multiple dimensions are explained, highlighting the gradient vector's computation using partial derivatives. A simple linear regression model is analyzed using a tips dataset, focusing on minimizing loss through MSE, complemented by visualization techniques such as heat maps and contour plots. Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is emphasized as a key algorithm with applications in Python.

children.title: Overview of Gradient Descent Discussion

children.title: Midterm Details and Review Session
children.summary: The midterm is scheduled for Wednesday, October 23rd, from 7 to 9 PM. Students should review the midterm logistics and weekly overview posts for complete details. A review session will take place on Friday in Evans 10 from 1:30 to 3:00 PM, with seating available on a first-come, first-serve basis (approximately 230 seats). Recordings and slides will be posted on Ed after the session. Seating assignments for the in-person midterm will be distributed on Monday, October 21st. Students needing accommodations due to extenuating circumstances are encouraged to fill out a designated form.

children.title: Gradient Descent Overview and Linear Regression Analysis
children.summary: The segment provides an overview of gradient descent optimization techniques, starting with the model and loss function using mean squared error (MSE) to minimize average loss. It covers the iterative update formula for adjusting model parameters and introduces concepts of gradient descent in one-dimensional and multi-dimensional contexts, including batch, mini-batch, and stochastic gradient descent. The importance of an initial guess and iterative refinement by subtracting a scaled gradient is discussed, along with visual representations of the MSE curve. The update rules for gradient descent in multiple dimensions are explained, detailing the computation of the gradient vector using partial derivatives. Additionally, the simple linear regression model is examined using a tips dataset, focusing on parameters theta zero and theta one, and emphasizes minimizing loss through MSE. Visualization techniques like heat maps and contour plots illustrate loss contours. Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is introduced as a key algorithm, with applications in Python, including the mean squared error gradient function.

children.title: Gradient Descent Optimization Techniques Overview
children.summary: The segment provides an overview of gradient descent optimization, starting with a recap of the model and loss function using mean squared error (MSE) to minimize average loss. It details the iterative update formula for adjusting model parameters and introduces gradient descent concepts in one-dimensional and multi-dimensional contexts, including batch, mini-batch, and stochastic gradient descent. The iterative algorithm is explained, focusing on selecting a model and loss function, computing the gradient, and updating parameters to reduce loss. The importance of an initial guess and iterative refinement by subtracting a scaled gradient is discussed, along with visual representations of the mean square error curve and model dimensionality. Additionally, the update rules for gradient descent in multiple dimensions are covered, explaining how to compute the gradient vector using partial derivatives of the loss function and extending the one-dimensional update rule to vector notation for multi-parameter models.

children.title: Gradient Descent Optimization Techniques Overview
children.summary: The segment covers gradient descent optimization, beginning with a recap of the model and loss function, specifically using mean squared error (MSE) to minimize average loss. It details the iterative update formula for adjusting model parameters and introduces concepts of gradient descent in one-dimensional and multi-dimensional contexts, including batch, mini-batch, and stochastic gradient descent. The iterative algorithm is explained, emphasizing the selection of a model and loss function, computing the gradient, and updating parameters to reduce loss. The importance of starting from an initial guess and iteratively refining it by subtracting a scaled gradient is discussed, along with visual representations of the mean square error curve and the dimensionality of models.

children.title: Gradient Descent Optimization Techniques
children.summary: The segment reviews the process of gradient descent optimization, beginning with a recap of the model and loss function used in the previous lecture. It emphasizes the steps involved in choosing a model and a loss function, specifically using mean squared error (MSE) as the loss. The focus is on minimizing the average loss through gradient descent, detailing the iterative update formula for adjusting model parameters. The discussion also introduces concepts related to gradient descent in both one-dimensional and multi-dimensional contexts, including batch, mini-batch, and stochastic gradient descent.

children.title: Gradient Descent Algorithm Overview
children.summary: The segment explains the iterative algorithm of gradient descent, which is used to minimize loss in a model. The process involves selecting a model, defining a loss function (specifically Mean Squared Error), and fitting the model by minimizing the average loss. The algorithm computes the gradient of the loss and updates the model parameters in the opposite direction of the gradient to gradually reduce the loss, akin to walking down a hill.

children.title: Gradient Descent Algorithm Explanation
children.summary: The segment discusses the gradient descent algorithm, starting from an initial guess and iteratively updating it by subtracting a scaled gradient. It emphasizes the importance of choosing a model and a loss function, specifically the Mean Squared Error (MSE). The process involves calculating the gradient and adjusting the guess until reaching a point where the gradient is zero, indicating a minimum. The visual representation of the mean square error curve is also mentioned, highlighting how guesses may not lie directly on the curve.

children.title: Dimensions in Model Optimization
children.summary: The discussion highlights the dimensionality of models, starting with one-dimensional models optimized using a single parameter (theta) to minimize loss. It progresses to two-dimensional models, mentioning parameters like intercept and slope, and extends to high-dimensional models, which can range from tens to thousands of dimensions, and even up to 400 billion in modern AI applications. The segment also introduces simple linear regression and multiple linear regression equations, emphasizing that most models require the optimization of multiple parameters.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: How many dimensions is this model? One dimension. Right, there's one parameter theta that I'll optimize it. Trying to minimize the loss with one parameter theta. But that's not fun. We want to do this for big models. Miles of maybe two dimensions. Is there? Intercept and maybe our slope. War. Really cool models with P dimensions. It's a very high-dimensional. And P could be, you know, in this class maybe 10. 20 I think it is something you might get a few thousand. Let me say 10,000 dimensions. I think it's pretty big. In the you know modern AIP can be. 400 billion dimensions. Video: 1: Previous Demo

children.title: Extending Derivatives to Multiple Dimensions
children.summary: The concept of derivatives and gradients is expanded to higher dimensions, particularly in the context of models with multiple parameters. Simple linear regression is represented as ŷ = β0 + β1X1, while multiple linear regression is expressed as ŷ = β0 + β1X1 + β2X2 + ... + βpXp. The idea is to enhance gradient descent to update all model parameters simultaneously. Additionally, the discussion introduces the concept of a loss surface, which evaluates the model's loss for different combinations of parameter values, emphasizing the importance of optimizing multiple parameters.

children.title: Optimization and Loss Surfaces in Learning Algorithms
children.summary: The focus is on optimization within learning algorithms, specifically aiming to reach the bottom of a loss curve. This involves understanding the model's loss for various parameter combinations, visualized as a loss surface in 2D or higher dimensions. The derivative of the loss function indicates the optimal direction towards the minimum, represented by the negative gradient vector, which guides the descent on the loss surface.

children.title: Understanding Gradient Vectors in Loss Functions
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the concept of moving along a two-dimensional surface to minimize a loss function. The derivative of the loss function indicates the optimal direction to move towards the minimum, described by the negative gradient vector. The segment emphasizes the importance of computing the gradient by taking the partial derivative of the loss with respect to each parameter. An active poll is also mentioned to engage participants in calculating the gradient.

children.title: Gradient Calculation for 2D Functions
children.summary: The discussion focuses on calculating the gradient of a 2D function, specifically f(θ_0, θ_1) = 80θ_0^2 + 300θ_1. The gradient is defined and expressed as a vector, with the audience participating in a poll to determine the correct gradient expression. The correct answer involves partial derivatives with respect to both variables, leading to a gradient vector that includes terms like 16θ_0 and 3θ_1.

children.title: Interpreting Gradients and Loss Function
children.summary: The segment discusses how to interpret gradients in the context of model parameters and loss. It explains that nudging model weights affects the loss, illustrating how the gradient indicates the direction to adjust parameters to minimize loss. The gradient vector is introduced, showing how to take partial derivatives of loss with respect to each parameter, and how it always points in the downhill direction on the loss surface.

children.title: Gradient Descent Update Rules in Multiple Dimensions
children.summary: The segment discusses the update rules for gradient descent in one-dimensional and multi-dimensional contexts. It explains how to compute the gradient vector by taking partial derivatives of the loss function with respect to each parameter and evaluating them at the current parameter values. The one-dimensional update rule is presented as A(t+1) = A(t) - α ∇L(A(t)), which is extended to vector notation for multi-parameter models, highlighting that the gradient vector always points in the downhill direction of the loss surface.

children.title: Gradient Descent Update Rules in Multiple Dimensions
children.summary: The segment discusses the update rules for gradient descent in both one-dimensional and multi-dimensional contexts. It explains how to compute the gradient vector by taking partial derivatives of the loss function with respect to each parameter and evaluating them at the current parameter values. The update rule for one-dimensional gradient descent is presented as A(t+1) = A(t) - α ∇L(A(t)), and for multi-parameter models, this is extended to vector notation, emphasizing that the gradient vector always points in the downhill direction of the loss surface.

children.title: Gradient Descent Update Rule and Mean Squared Error
children.summary: The segment discusses the loss function in optimization, focusing on the gradient descent update rule for models with multiple parameters. It introduces the update formula θ(t+1) = θ(t) - α * ∇L(θ(t)), where ∇L is the gradient of the loss function. The model is specified as f(x) = θ0x0 + θ1x1, with the loss function defined as l(y, g) = (y - g)². The mean squared error is emphasized as a loss function, detailing the gradient computation using the chain rule. The process of nudging parameters in the negative gradient direction until convergence is explained, illustrating the practical application of the gradient descent update rule.

children.title: Gradient Descent Update Rule in Multiple Dimensions
children.summary: The segment discusses the concept of loss in optimization, emphasizing that while any function can be optimized, the focus is on the loss function, which is typically represented as an average in modeling tasks. It introduces the gradient descent update rule for models with multiple parameters, explaining the update formula in vector terms and illustrating how to compute the next value by nudging the parameters in the negative gradient direction until convergence is achieved. The relationship between the current parameters and the gradient of the loss function is highlighted.

children.title: Gradient Descent Update Rule and Model Definition
children.summary: The gradient descent algorithm is introduced, which involves nudging the parameter vector θ in the negative gradient direction until convergence is achieved. The update rule for θ is defined as θ(t+1) = θ(t) - α * ∇L(θ(t)), where ∇L is the gradient of the loss function evaluated at the current θ. The model is specified as f(x) = θ0x0 + θ1x1, and the loss function is defined as l(y, g) = (y - g)², where g represents the model's output.

children.title: Mean Squared Error and Gradient Descent
children.summary: The discussion covers the mean squared error as a loss function, emphasizing the computation of its gradient using the chain rule. It details the process of calculating the gradient for the parameters, specifically focusing on how to derive the update rule for gradient descent. The gradient descent algorithm is explained as nudging the parameters in the negative gradient direction until convergence is achieved. Additionally, a specific model and loss function are presented, illustrating how to apply the gradient descent update rule in practice.

children.title: Stopping Conditions and Gradient Descent in Linear Regression
children.summary: Two common stopping conditions for gradient descent are discussed: running the algorithm for a fixed number of steps (e.g., 10,000) and stopping when updates become minimal, indicating convergence. The algorithm updates parameters in the negative gradient direction until convergence is reached, which can be defined by minimal updates or a set number of iterations. The application of gradient descent to multi-dimensional models is illustrated through predicting tips based on total bill amounts, incorporating a bias term. The mean squared error (MSE) is introduced as the loss function to optimize model parameters, represented as a linear equation with two parameters. Additionally, the gradient descent method is explained for optimizing parameters in a simple linear regression model, including an intercept and slope term, with a focus on minimizing the MSE and visualizing the loss surface.

children.title: Stopping Conditions for Gradient Descent
children.summary: Two common stopping conditions for gradient descent are discussed: running the iterative procedure for a fixed number of steps, such as 10,000, and stopping when updates become minimal, indicating convergence. The gradient descent algorithm nudges the parameter in the negative gradient direction until it converges, which occurs when subsequent updates do not change the parameter significantly or at all. The loss function and model specifics are outlined, emphasizing the importance of monitoring changes to determine when to halt the algorithm.

children.title: Gradient Descent and Multi-Dimensional Models
children.summary: The gradient descent algorithm updates parameters in the negative gradient direction until convergence, defined as when updates become minimal or a fixed number of iterations is reached. The discussion includes applying gradient descent to multi-dimensional models, specifically for predicting tips based on total bill amounts with a bias term. The mean squared error (MSE) function is introduced to optimize the model parameters, which are represented as a linear equation with two parameters.

Notebook [7
Gradient Descent on Multi-Dimensional Models
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
Now suppose we improve our model so that we want to predict the tip from the total_bill plus a constant offset, in other words:
tip = 00+01bill
Our simple linear regression model has two parameters, 00 and 01. We need to optimize both of them.
Fortunately, gradient descent readily extends to models with multiple dimenions.
Defining a 2D MSE Function
Now, we can give our predictions as
§ = 00 + 01x1
In the cell below, we add a bias term to our data to represent the constant intercept 00.
[ ]:
tips_with_bias = df.copy( )
tips_with_bias ["bias"] = 1
tips_with_bias = tips_with_bias [ ["bias", "total_bill"] ]
tips_with_bias.head( )
[ ]:
X = tips_with_bias
y = df ["tip"]
Throughout this problem, we'll assume we want to minimize the mean squared error of our predictions, i.e.
[ ]: def mse_loss (theta):
y_hat = X @ theta
return np.mean((y - y_hat) ** 2)
Using this function, we can visualize our loss function. Because we now want to understand how the loss changes with respect to two parameters, we create a loss surface. Each point on the surface
represents the loss of the model for a particular choice of 00 and 01.
The cell below uses lots of syntax you've never seen. No need to worry about any unfamiliar plotting code; for now, focus on the output.

Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
Now suppose we improve our model so that we want to predict the tip from the total_bill plus a constant offset, in other words:
tip = 00+01bill
Our simple linear regression model has two parameters, 00 and 01. We need to optimize both of them.
Fortunately, gradient descent readily extends to models with multiple dimenions.
Defining a 2D MSE Function
Now, we can give our predictions as
§ = 00 + 01x1
In the cell below, we add a bias term to our data to represent the constant intercept 00.

y_hat = X @ theta
return np.mean((y - y_hat) ** 2)
Using this function, we can visualize our loss function. Because we now want to understand how the loss changes with respect to two parameters, we create a loss surface. Each point on the surface
represents the loss of the model for a particular choice of 00 and 01.

children.title: Gradient Descent in Linear Regression
children.summary: The gradient descent algorithm is introduced as a method to optimize the parameters of a simple linear regression model, which includes an intercept term (theta zero) and a slope term (theta one). The algorithm updates the parameters in the negative gradient direction of the loss function until convergence is achieved, defined as either reaching a fixed number of updates or when subsequent updates do not significantly change the parameters. The mean squared error (MSE) is used as the loss function to be minimized, and a bias term is added to the data to represent the constant intercept. A visualization of the loss surface is discussed, showing how the loss varies with different parameter choices.

children.title: Linear Regression, Stochastic Gradient Descent, and Complexity Analysis
children.summary: The segment discusses the simple linear regression model using a tips dataset, focusing on parameters theta zero (intercept) and theta one (slope). It explains the loss function concept, emphasizing the goal of minimizing loss through the mean squared error (MSE) function, including the bias term and MSE structure. Visualization techniques like heat maps and contour plots illustrate loss contours with brighter colors indicating higher loss. Gradient descent for multi-dimensional models is introduced, detailing the update rule and deriving the gradient for MSE, along with the importance of defining the gradient for optimization and the Minimize package for model optimization. Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is introduced as a key algorithm, highlighting its application in Python, including the mean squared error gradient function and SGD function, and its ability to handle multi-dimensional models with batch size and iterative updates. The computational efficiency of gradient descent is analyzed, revealing a minimum work of O(nd) and an overall complexity of O(Tnd) for T iterations, exemplified by a scenario with 10 million entries and 100 dimensions. The segment also covers using probability sampling in SGD to approximate the gradient by selecting a sample of B data points randomly without replacement to compute an average gradient.

children.title: Linear Regression Loss, Visualization, and Optimization
children.summary: The segment covers the simple linear regression model using a tips dataset, focusing on the parameters theta zero (intercept) and theta one (slope). It explains the loss function concept, emphasizing the goal of minimizing loss, and introduces the mean squared error (MSE) function, including the bias term and MSE structure. Visualization techniques such as heat maps and contour plots are discussed, illustrating loss contours where brighter colors indicate higher loss. The segment also introduces gradient descent for multi-dimensional models, detailing the update rule and deriving the gradient for the MSE. Finally, it emphasizes the importance of defining the gradient for optimization and introduces the Minimize package for model optimization.

children.title: Understanding Linear Regression Loss Function
children.summary: The segment discusses a simple linear regression model using a tips dataset, highlighting the two parameters: theta zero (intercept) and theta one (slope). It explains the concept of a loss function, where each point on a surface represents the loss for a specific model choice, and emphasizes the goal of minimizing this loss. The segment also introduces the mean squared error (MSE) function for predictions, detailing the addition of a bias term to the dataset and the structure of the MSE loss function.

import plotly. graph_objects as go
uvalues = np. linspace(-1, 5, 20)
vvalues = np. linspace(-0.1, 0.35, 20)
(u,v) = np.meshgrid(uvalues, vvalues)
thetas = np.vstack((u.flatten( ), v. flatten()))
MSE = np.array( [mse_loss(t) for t in thetas.T] )
loss_surface = go. Surface (x=u,
y=v, z=np. reshape (MSE, u. shape),
contours = {"z": {"show": True, "start": 0, "end": 50, "size": 2, "color": "white"}} )
# This is an approximate guess for the optimal point.
ind = np.argmin (MSE)
optimal_point = go. Scatter3d(name = "Optimal Point",
x = [thetas. T [ind, 0]], y = [thetas. T [ind, 1] ] ,
z = [MSE [ind] ] ,
marker=dict(size=10, color="red") )
fig = go. Figure(data=[loss_surface, optimal_point] )
fig. update_layout (scene = dict(
xaxis_title = "theta0",
yaxis_title = "theta1",
zaxis_title = "MSE"), autosize=False, width=800, height=600)
fig. show( )

import plotly. graph_objects as go
uvalues = np. linspace(-1, 5, 20)
vvalues = np. linspace(-0.1, 0.35, 20)
(u,v) = np.meshgrid(uvalues, vvalues)
thetas = np.vstack((u.flatten( ), v. flatten()))
MSE = np.array( [mse_loss(t) for t in thetas.T] )
loss_surface = go. Surface (x=u,
y=v, z=np. reshape (MSE, u. shape),
contours = {"z": {"show": True, "start": 0, "end": 50, "size": 2, "color": "white"}} )
# This is an approximate guess for the optimal point.
ind = np.argmin (MSE)
optimal_point = go. Scatter3d(name = "Optimal Point",
x = [thetas. T [ind, 0]], y = [thetas. T [ind, 1] ] ,
z = [MSE [ind] ] ,
marker=dict(size=10, color="red") )
fig = go. Figure(data=[loss_surface, optimal_point] )
fig. update_layout (scene = dict(
xaxis_title = "theta0",
yaxis_title = "theta1",
zaxis_title = "MSE"), autosize=False, width=800, height=600)
fig. show( )

MSE = np.array( [mse_loss(t) for t in thetas.T] )
loss_surface = go. Surface (x=u,
y=v, z=np. reshape (MSE, u. shape),
contours = {"z": {"show": True, "start": 0, "end": 50, "size": 2, "color": "white"}} )
# This is an approximate guess for the optimal point.
ind = np.argmin (MSE)
optimal_point = go. Scatter3d(name = "Optimal Point",
x = [thetas. T [ind, 0]], y = [thetas. T [ind, 1] ] ,
z = [MSE [ind] ] ,
marker=dict(size=10, color="red") )
fig = go. Figure(data=[loss_surface, optimal_point] )
fig. update_layout (scene = dict(
xaxis_title = "theta0",

fig. show( )

children.title: Visualizing Loss with Heat Maps and Contour Plots
children.summary: Heat maps are used to visualize loss contours, with brighter colors indicating higher loss and darker colors indicating lower loss. In high-dimensional data, visualizing loss becomes challenging, but the focus will be on multidimensional models. A contour plot can provide a bird's-eye view of the loss surface, showing the lowest point marked in red, which corresponds to the parameters yielding the lowest mean squared error (MSE). The update rule for gradient descent in multidimensional models is introduced, emphasizing the gradient of the loss function as a vector of partial derivatives. The derivation of the gradient for the MSE is outlined, leading to the computation of the partial derivatives.

children.title: Gradient Descent and Loss Function Derivation
children.summary: The segment discusses the derivation of gradients for a loss function in a multi-dimensional model, applying gradient descent with a learning rate. It highlights the update rule for parameters and provides the final model parameters obtained after running the algorithm 10,000 times. The equations for the loss function and its gradient are presented, emphasizing the use of the chain rule in the derivation.

In the cell below, we define helper functions to compute the gradient of MSE with respect to our two parameters 00 and 01, stored in the array theta .
[52]:
def mse_gradient (theta):
"""Returns the gradient of the MSE on our data for the given theta"""
#x0 = X.iloc[ :, 0] constant term (1)
x1 = X. iloc[ :, 1]
dth0 = np.mean(32 * (y - (theta[0] + theta[1]*x1)))
dth1 = np.mean(-2* (y- (theta[0] + theta[1]*x1)) *x1)
return np. array ( [dth0, dth1])
Now, we can use our gradient_descent function from before to optimize 00 and 01 at the same time! The final estimates for the ideal model parameters are very similar to the guesses we may have made
simply by inspecting the plot of the loss surface from before.
The cell below may take a moment to run.
[ ]:
guesses = gradient_descent (mse_gradient, np.array( [1, . 5]), 0.001, 10000)
pd. DataFrame (guesses, columns=["theta_0", "theta_1"] ).tail (10)
Comparing with the scipy minimize function.
[ ]: minimize(mse_loss, x0 = [0,0])
We can actually provide the gradient information to the scipy optimizer to get an even faster solution.
[ ]: minimize(mse_loss, x0 = [0,0],jac=mse_gradient)

def mse_gradient (theta):
"""Returns the gradient of the MSE on our data for the given theta"""
#x0 = X.iloc[ :, 0] constant term (1)
x1 = X. iloc[ :, 1]
dth0 = np.mean(-2 * (y - (theta[0] + theta[1]*x1)))
dth1 = np.mean(-2 * (y - (theta[0] ++ theta[1]*x1)) * x1)
return np. array ( [dth0, dth1] )
Now, we can use our gradient_descent function from before to optimize 00 and 01 at the same time! The final estimates for the ideal model parameters are very similar to the guesses we may have made
simply by inspecting the plot of the loss surface from before.

children.title: Gradient Optimization and Minimize Package
children.summary: The discussion focuses on defining the gradient with respect to the vector theta, emphasizing its importance for model optimization. A package called Minimize is introduced, which estimates gradients by sampling data points on the surface to approximate the curvature, facilitating gradient descent optimization. This package can significantly reduce the number of iterations needed for convergence, especially with large datasets. The importance of setting parameters like alpha and monitoring convergence during optimization routines is highlighted. Additionally, the use of a gradient_descent function is mentioned for optimizing model parameters, with final estimates aligning closely with initial visual inspections of the loss surface.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And again, all you need to do that was to define a gradient respect to my vector theta. for this month. And that is a really powerful tool. So you call it a crazy model as long as you can take those derivatives. You can optimize that model and fit it to your data. So last lecture, I talked about a package called Minimize. It's very sophisticated. It tries to estimate the gradient by sampling the data, sampling points on the surface, sort of like measuring the curving, like I'll take a few points here, maybe I'll put a plane to it and I get a guess what the gradient looks like. That function... Also has green descent built into it. So you can construct your gradient function. You can pass that in. I'll take far fewer iterations to solve the problem. Which is good because if this data was big this could take a long time. So this gives you another way to optimize. In practice, you'll probably do this, or you'll suffer packages that run gradient descent. The reason I'm telling you all of this stuff is that you'll need to set parameters like this alpha and you'd look at things like whether or not it's converging as you're writing that up from station routine. And now you have an intuition of what's going on when we do that. Video: 1: File

In the cell below, we define helper functions to compute the gradient of MSE with respect to our two parameters 00 and 01, stored in the array theta .

[53]:
def mse_gradient (theta) :
"""Returns the gradient of the MSE on our data for the given theta"""
#x0 = X.iloc[ :, 0] constant term (1)
x1 = X. iloc[ :, 1]
dth0 = np.mean(-2 * (y - (theta[0] + theta[1]*x1)) * 1)
dth1 = np.mean(-2 * (y - (theta[0]+ theta[1]*x1)) * x1)
return np. array ( [dth0, dth1] )
Now, we can use our gradient_descent function from before to optimize 00 and 01 at the same time! The final estimates for the ideal model parameters are very similar to the guesses we may have made
simply by inspecting the plot of the loss surface from before.

leaf_chunk.audio: And again, all you need to do that was to define a gradient respect to my vector theta. for this month. And that is a really powerful tool. So you call it a crazy model as long as you can take those derivatives. You can optimize that model and fit it to your data. So last lecture, I talked about a package called Minimize. It's very sophisticated. It tries to estimate the gradient by sampling the data, sampling points on the surface, sort of like measuring the curving, like I'll take a few points here, maybe I'll put a plane to it and I get a guess what the gradient looks like. That function... Also has green descent built into it. So you can construct your gradient function. You can pass that in. I'll take far fewer iterations to solve the problem. Which is good because if this data was big this could take a long time. So this gives you another way to optimize. In practice, you'll probably do this, or you'll suffer packages that run gradient descent. The reason I'm telling you all of this stuff is that you'll need to set parameters like this alpha and you'd look at things like whether or not it's converging as you're writing that up from station routine. And now you have an intuition of what's going on when we do that.

def mse_gradient (theta) :
"""Returns the gradient of the MSE on our data for the given theta"""
#x0 = X.iloc[ :, 0] constant term (1)
x1 = X. iloc[ :, 1]

dth1 = np.mean(-2 * (y - (theta[0]+ theta[1]*x1)) * x1)

children.title: Introduction to Stochastic Gradient Descent and Complexity Analysis
children.summary: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is introduced as a key algorithm for 2024, with emphasis on its application and implementation in Python, including the mean squared error gradient function and SGD function. The segment discusses how SGD can handle multi-dimensional models, focusing on batch size and iterative updates to parameters. It also covers gradient descent as a method for optimizing the loss function, addressing its computational efficiency and complexities. The computational effort is analyzed, revealing that with n entries and D-dimensional vectors, the minimum work is O(nd), leading to an overall complexity of O(Tnd) for T iterations. An example highlights that with 10 million entries and 100 dimensions, the complexity scales linearly with the number of iterations.

children.title: Introduction to Stochastic Gradient Descent
children.summary: The segment introduces Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) as a significant algorithm for 2024, highlighting its relevance and encouraging excitement about its application. It briefly mentions the context of the problem being addressed and transitions into discussing the implementation of SGD in Python, including the definition of the mean squared error gradient function and the SGD function itself. The segment outlines how SGD can operate on multi-dimensional models, emphasizing concepts like batch size and iterative updates to the parameter theta.

[ ]: def mse_gradient (theta, X, y):
"""Returns the gradient of the MSE on our data for the given theta"""
x0 = X. iloc[ :, 0]
x1 = X. iloc[ :, 1]
dth0 = np.mean(-2 * (y - theta[0]*x0 - theta[1]*x1) * x0)
dth1 = np.mean(-2 * (y - theta[0]*x0 - theta[1]*x1) *x1)
return np. array ( [dth0, dth1])
[ ]: def sgd(grad, X, y, initial_theta, eta = 0.3, max_iter = 5000, batch_size=50 ):
theta = initial_theta
thetas = [theta]
n = len (X)
for t in range(1, max_iter) :
X_sample = X.sample(batch_size)
y_sample = y. loc [X_sample. index]
theta = theta - eta/t * grad(theta, X_sample, y_sample)
thetas. append (theta)
return thetas
thetas = sadimse gradient. X. v.

theta = theta - eta/t * grad(theta, X_sample, y_sample)
thetas. append (theta)
return thetas
thetas = sadimse gradient. X. v.

children.title: Complexity Analysis of Gradient Descent
children.summary: The segment discusses the computational complexity of the gradient descent algorithm, specifically highlighting the formula O(Tnd) where n represents the number of data points, d denotes the number of dimensions, and T is the number of iterations. It emphasizes that typically T is much smaller than d, which in turn is much smaller than n. The discussion also raises the question of whether the cost of this algorithm can be reduced using techniques from Data100.

children.title: Complexity of Gradient Descent and Sampling
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the complexity of the gradient descent algorithm, highlighting its computational cost of O(nd) for n data points and d dimensions. It emphasizes that typically T, the number of iterations, is much smaller than both d and n. The segment also raises the question of whether the cost of the algorithm can be reduced using techniques learned in Data100, while briefly touching on the concept of sampling and averaging data points.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent and Gradient Approximation
children.summary: The segment covers the use of probability sampling to approximate the gradient in stochastic gradient descent (SGD). It explains that a sample of B data points can be selected randomly without replacement to compute an average gradient, providing an effective estimate of the true gradient. The mathematical expression of the true gradient is presented, emphasizing the significance of using 'b' records for estimation. The discussion highlights the noisy nature of gradients from individual data points and suggests that increasing the mini-batch size improves gradient estimates and consistency in minimizing loss. Additionally, it notes that larger mini-batch sizes enhance computational efficiency, allowing parallel processing on multiple GPUs, contrasting with the original SGD which used a batch size of one.

children.title: Approximating Gradient with Probability Sampling
children.summary: A probability sample can be used to approximate the gradient in stochastic gradient descent. By selecting a sample of B data points from the dataset, the gradient is computed as an average over these points. The process involves taking random samples without replacement and calculating the gradient based on these selected indices, which provides an approximation to the true gradient.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent: Approximating the Gradient
children.summary: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is introduced as a method to approximate the true gradient using a probability sample. The true gradient is expressed mathematically, and it is emphasized that by sampling 'b' records, one can estimate the gradient effectively. The presentation includes various representations of the gradient and highlights the use of indices to select the sampled records.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent Overview
children.summary: The segment discusses the stochastic gradient descent algorithm, which involves taking random data points to determine the direction for minimizing loss. It highlights the noisy nature of individual data point gradients and suggests that increasing the batch size can provide a better estimate of the gradient, leading to more consistent movement towards the minimum. The concept of using a probability sample to approximate the gradient is introduced, emphasizing the importance of mini-batch size in this process.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Why don't we do? One. This was the original stochastic grain descent algorithm. Take one data point out of all of my data. Which way does that data point think I should move if I want to minimize the loss on just that data point? Take a tiny step in that direction. Take another random data point, you know what it says go backwards okay well that's a bummer i take another point Let's go this way a little bit On average, all these data points are going to make little steps wandering all over the surface, but eventually... Hopefully. They will take me to the bottom. But I just made the come. that costed during this much smaller. Right, but they're really noisy. How could I make it less noisy? children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent and Mini-batch Size
children.summary: The choice of mini-batch size (b) in Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) affects the gradient estimation and computational efficiency. A larger b provides a better gradient estimate and allows for parallel processing across multiple GPUs, as each can compute its own gradient. The original SGD algorithm uses b=1, but modern practices often utilize larger values, typically small in relation to the dataset size, to balance accuracy and computational workload.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So, my choice would be actually ends up depending on several factors. Oh no. In practice, we're going to use a slightly larger B to get a better gradient estimate. And this is exciting to use our GPUs. We make B bigger, there's more work to do. It can be done entirely in parallel. Each GPU can compute its own little separate gradient. children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent: Gradient Sampling and Mini-Batch Size
children.summary: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) utilizes probability samples to approximate the gradient, allowing for better estimates of the true gradient. The mini-batch size, typically small, is denoted as 'b' and influences the gradient estimation and computational efficiency. A larger mini-batch size can enhance parallelism and improve gradient estimates, but it also experiences rapidly diminishing returns. The choice of mini-batch size depends on various factors, including the specific requirements of the system.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent and Mini-Batch Processing
children.summary: The segment covers the implementation of Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) in Python, focusing on initializing the parameter 'theta' and performing multiple epochs over the dataset. It emphasizes the necessity of shuffling both features (X) and labels (Y) to maintain their correspondence. The discussion includes the process of iterating through data in mini-batches, updating 'theta' based on computed gradients, and the efficiency of using mini-batches (e.g., B=10) for quicker gradient computation. The importance of shuffling data to ensure randomness and enhance performance, particularly with large datasets, is also highlighted, along with a sample code snippet demonstrating the implementation of SGD.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent Overview
children.summary: The segment discusses the implementation of Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) in Python, emphasizing the importance of initializing the parameter 'theta' and performing multiple epochs over the dataset. It highlights the necessity of shuffling the data together for both features (X) and labels (Y) to maintain their correspondence. The provided code snippet outlines the process, including iterating through the data in mini-batches and updating 'theta' based on the computed gradients.

children.title: Stochastic Mini-Batch Gradient Descent Overview
children.summary: The segment discusses stochastic gradient descent, specifically focusing on mini-batch processing. It explains the formula for updating parameters, where new theta is calculated by subtracting a fraction of the gradient from the old theta. The speaker emphasizes the efficiency of using a batch of data (e.g., B=10) instead of the entire dataset, allowing for quicker gradient computation. The importance of shuffling data to ensure randomness in sampling for each step is also highlighted, along with the overall process of iterating through the data in epochs.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent and Data Shuffling
children.summary: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is introduced as a method to estimate the true gradient of the loss surface using a sample of data, which allows for quicker gradient computation. The process involves shuffling the data and then performing linear traversal to efficiently manage memory and speed up computations. The lecture highlights the importance of shuffling data to enhance performance, especially when dealing with large datasets. A sample code snippet illustrates the implementation of SGD, including shuffling and updating the model parameters across multiple epochs.

leaf_chunk.text:

There's some problems. These are more technical problems, computational problems. If I keep drawing a random sample from an enormous data set, I'm going out there and collecting records all over memory. And that's slow. So I'd like to shuffle my data and then just walk down my data one record at a time the computer is very happy with this kind of linear traversal my data. And so we tend to do that. I will confess. children.title: Data Shuffling in Stochastic Gradient Descent
children.summary: Data shuffling in stochastic gradient descent is typically done once before iterating through the data. This method resembles simple random sampling with replacement but can be costly due to multiple data fetches. While some may opt not to shuffle, it is generally recommended to avoid bias from ordered data. However, excessive shuffling does not significantly improve convergence.

children.title: Data Shuffling in Stochastic Gradient Descent
children.summary: In practice, data shuffling is often performed once before iterating through the data in stochastic gradient descent. This method allows for sampling akin to a simple random sample with replacement, though it can be costly due to the need to fetch data multiple times. While some practitioners may choose not to shuffle their data, it is generally advisable to do so, especially if the data is ordered in a way that could bias results. However, excessive shuffling does not significantly enhance convergence and may not be necessary.

It says that I took a bunch of random samples. Other questions? Now back. Correct. It's as though I'm sampling a simple random sample with replacement, but again, it's it's kind of costly. I have to go fetch a data kind of one use it once. I want to make sure I get down my data. So in practice, people often shuffle their data once and then just loop over it. Some people don't even shuffle their data. Which can be okay if your data is in kind of an arbitrary order it could be a terrible idea if your data was sorted by like glued records bad records. children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent Techniques and Midterm Overview
children.summary: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is introduced as a method for estimating the gradient of the loss surface using data samples, which allows for faster computations through data shuffling and batch selection. Unlike Batch Gradient Descent, SGD's approximative nature may prevent it from reaching the true minimum. A code snippet illustrates the SGD algorithm, covering parameter initialization and iterative updates. The concept of epochs is explained, defining one epoch as a complete pass over the dataset, resulting in 8,192 gradient updates with a batch size of 1. The importance of multiple epochs for effective training with large datasets is emphasized. Additionally, an overview of midterm exam content is provided, highlighting the need to review all assignments and noting the relevance of some material for the final exam, particularly St. Caster Gray's algorithm.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent Techniques and Midterm Overview
children.summary: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is introduced as a method for estimating the gradient of the loss surface using data samples, allowing for faster computations through data shuffling and batch selection. Unlike Batch Gradient Descent, which consistently approaches the true minimum, SGD's approximative nature may prevent it from reaching the minimum. A code snippet illustrates the SGD algorithm, including parameter initialization and iterative updates. The segment explains the concept of epochs, defining one epoch as a complete pass over the dataset, resulting in 8,192 gradient updates when using a batch size of 1. The importance of multiple epochs for effective training with large datasets is emphasized. Additionally, an overview of midterm exam content is provided, stressing the need to review all assignments and noting the relevance of some material for the final exam, along with highlighting St. Caster Gray's algorithm as significant.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent Overview and Techniques
children.summary: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is introduced as a method that estimates the true gradient of the loss surface using data samples, enabling faster gradient computations through data shuffling and batch selection. In contrast to Batch Gradient Descent, which consistently moves towards the true minimum, SGD's approximative nature means it may not always reach the minimum. A code snippet illustrates the SGD algorithm, detailing parameter initialization, data shuffling, and iterative model updates. The segment also compares the two techniques, emphasizing that while Batch Gradient Descent computes the true gradient, SGD with a batch size of 1 approximates it. An example fitting an Ordinary Least Squares model to 8192 data points using SGD raises a question about the number of gradient updates per epoch, leading to a clarification that the initial suggestion of 'one' is incorrect.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent Overview and Comparison
children.summary: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) estimates the true gradient of the loss surface using a sample of data, allowing for quicker gradient computations. The process involves shuffling data and selecting batches for each gradient calculation. In contrast, traditional Batch Gradient Descent computes the true gradient and consistently descends towards the true minimum of the loss, whereas SGD may not always achieve this due to its approximative nature. The segment includes a code snippet illustrating the SGD algorithm, highlighting the steps of initializing parameters, shuffling data, and updating the model iteratively.

children.title: Gradient Descent Techniques and Updates
children.summary: The discussion focuses on gradient descent techniques, specifically comparing batch gradient descent and stochastic gradient descent (SGD). It highlights that while batch gradient descent computes the true gradient and consistently moves towards the true minimum of the loss function, stochastic gradient descent, particularly with a batch size of 1, approximates the true gradient and may not always move towards the true minimum with each update. An example is provided where an Ordinary Least Squares model is fitted to a dataset of 8192 data points using SGD, prompting a question about the number of gradient updates per epoch.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It's a random walk to the bottom. But it's a really fast random walk and that's the important point because i can use this sampling to go much much much faster. A more slider. children.title: Gradient Updates in Stochastic Gradient Descent
children.summary: The discussion focuses on fitting an Ordinary Least Squares model to a dataset with 8,192 data points using stochastic gradient descent with a batch size of 1. The question posed is about the number of gradient updates per epoch. Initially, the answer 'one' is suggested, but it is clarified that this is incorrect for stochastic gradient descent, as each data point results in a gradient update, leading to a total of 8,192 updates per epoch.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent and Epochs Explained
children.summary: This segment explains stochastic gradient descent (SGD) in the context of an Ordinary Least Squares model using 8,192 data points. It contrasts SGD, which updates the model using one data point at a time, with vanilla gradient descent that uses all data points for a single gradient computation. An epoch is defined as one complete pass over the dataset, resulting in 8,192 gradient updates per epoch with a batch size of 1. The discussion highlights the importance of multiple epochs for thorough training, especially with large datasets, and details how the averaging of gradients from individual data points contributes to the optimization process.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent Explanation and Polls
children.summary: The discussion focuses on stochastic gradient descent (SGD) using an Ordinary Least Squares model with 8,192 data points. It contrasts vanilla gradient descent, which uses all data points to construct a gradient for one step, with SGD, which updates the model using one data point at a time. The segment includes a series of active polls asking participants how many gradient updates occur per epoch when using a batch size of 1 in SGD.

children.title: Understanding Epochs and Gradient Updates
children.summary: Epoch refers to one complete pass over the dataset during training. In the context of using stochastic gradient descent with a batch size of 1, each data point results in a gradient update. Thus, for a dataset with 8192 data points, there will be 8192 gradient updates per epoch. The choice of batch size affects the number of gradient descent steps taken within each epoch.

children.title: Multiple Epochs and Batch Size in Training
children.summary: Training models like ChatGPT typically involves multiple epochs due to the vast amount of data, such as trillions of data points, while in this class, students will work with 10,000 data points. Each epoch consists of several steps, and it's common to take more than one epoch to ensure thorough training. Additionally, when using stochastic gradient descent with a batch size of one, each data point contributes to the gradient computation, and the average of these gradients is used to take a single optimization step.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent and Gradient Calculation
children.summary: The process of using stochastic gradient descent with a batch size of 1 is discussed, specifically in the context of fitting an Ordinary Least Squares model to a dataset of 8192 data points. The procedure involves taking two data points, computing their gradients, and averaging these to approximate the true gradient at that point. The segment also mentions the frequency of gradient updates per epoch, emphasizing the iterative nature of the algorithm.

children.title: Batch Size Effects in Stochastic Gradient Descent
children.summary: Smaller batch sizes lead to more frequent gradient updates but with higher variability, while larger batch sizes provide more stable and higher quality gradient updates, albeit less frequently. Choosing an appropriate batch size is essential for hardware efficiency and compute resources. Many software packages will automatically select these parameters, but users may need to tune them. Additionally, an example of stochastic gradient descent (SGD) implementation in PyTorch is provided, emphasizing the use of data loaders and the importance of computing gradients and updating model parameters.

So, smaller batch size, more frequent up gradient updates, more variability in the gradient estimates and bouncing over the place but I'm going fast. larger batch sizes i get more under acceleration if i make it bigger i get higher quality green updates to like more likely in the right direction But I do it less frequently because I'm doing all the sexier compute. To forgot which way is down. Again, the typical solution. So I'm going to choose this batch size to ensure a hardware efficiency, compute, and so on. The good news is a lot of the software packages you will use will pick these for you. But you might need to tune them. so you need to know about these parameters. children.title: Gradient Computation and Batch Size Tradeoffs
children.summary: The segment discusses the use of PyTorch for defining loss functions and models in Python, emphasizing the ease of automatic gradient computation and efficiency, especially with GPU utilization. It outlines the tradeoffs of using smaller versus larger batch sizes in training: smaller batches provide more frequent gradient updates but with higher variability, while larger batches leverage hardware acceleration for better performance and higher quality updates but result in less frequent updates. A typical solution is to set the batch size to optimize hardware utilization. Additionally, a brief code example for Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) in PyTorch is provided, highlighting the use of DataLoader and the importance of computing loss and gradients.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent in PyTorch Overview
children.summary: The segment introduces a simple implementation of Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) in PyTorch, detailing a function 'sgd' that takes a model, loss function, dataset, learning rate, number of epochs, and batch size as parameters. It describes how to create a DataLoader for batching, compute the loss using the model's predictions, and perform backpropagation to update model parameters. The discussion emphasizes that in practice, software packages are used to handle gradient computations and SGD updates.

children.title: Gradient Descent and Sampling Strategies in SGD
children.summary: The segment discusses the implementation of gradient descent, specifically the CASTIC gradient descent and its abbreviated form, SGD. It outlines the parameters such as initial theta, learning rate, maximum iterations (set to 5,000), and batch size (50). The lecture emphasizes the importance of sampling data correctly, ensuring that the corresponding Y values are selected with the same indices as the X values. Additionally, a brief introduction to using PyTorch for SGD is provided, explaining how to set up a data loader, compute loss, and update model parameters using gradient descent.

"""Returns the gradient of the MSE on our data for the given theta"""

dth1 = np.mean(-2 * (y - theta[0]*x0 - theta[1]*x1) * x1)

thetas. append (theta)

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent Implementation and Concepts
children.summary: The segment discusses the implementation of stochastic gradient descent (SGD) for optimizing multi-dimensional models. It highlights the adjustment of the learning rate to prevent overshooting during updates. A code snippet for calculating the mean squared error (MSE) gradient is provided, along with a function for performing SGD. The function samples data in batches and updates the parameters iteratively. The speaker mentions the absence of a visualization but plans to provide one later to illustrate the sample path of the optimization process.

Audio: Theta, oh I have a little bit fancier learning right here so the learning rate I'm making my learning rate smaller with each step so I don't overshoot. I will talk about my radio. On that sample. Evaluated at the current data. Let's run it. Why don't I have a visualization? That's a bummer. Alright, I'll try to post-visualization after lecture. It shows you the sample path as it moves this space. Which you can see it's going to jump around and get to a similar answer to the one we got from before running gradient descent. We're going to make many, many more steps. I think we've talked enough about stochastic gradient. Video: 1: File

children.title: Midterm Content and Key Algorithms Overview
children.summary: The segment provides an overview of the content necessary for the midterm exam, emphasizing the importance of reviewing all assignments. It also notes that some material may be relevant for the final exam. Additionally, St. Caster Gray's algorithm is highlighted as a significant algorithm of the 2020s, utilized for training large neural networks and foundational to current generative AI technologies.

children.title: Midterm Content Overview and Key Algorithms
children.summary: The segment concludes the material necessary for the midterm exam, indicating that students should review all assignments as well. It highlights that certain content may appear on the final exam. The discussion includes a mention of St. Caster Gray's algorithm, described as the hottest algorithm of the 2020s, which is used to train massive neural networks and underpins current generative AI technologies.

return np. array ( [dth0, dth1])

def sgd(grad, X, y, initial_theta, eta = 0.3, max_iter = 5000, batch_size=50 ):
theta = initial_theta
thetas = [theta]
n = len (X)
for t in range(1, max_iter) :
X_sample = X.sample(batch_size)
y_sample = y. loc [X_sample. index]
theta = theta - eta/t * grad(theta, X_sample, y_sample)
thetas. append (theta)
return thetas

children.title: Epochs, Gradient Descent, and Stochastic Techniques
children.summary: The segment clarifies the concept of an epoch in gradient descent, explaining that it involves using the entire dataset to compute a single gradient before taking a step in optimization. It introduces stochastic gradient descent (SGD), where a sample from the dataset is used to compute the gradient, followed by taking steps iteratively with different blocks of data. The discussion includes the parameter 'B' and its impact on the number of steps in the optimization process. The iterative nature of updating parameters is emphasized, with multiple techniques for comparing gradient descent methods mentioned. Additionally, the importance of shuffling data in sampling is highlighted to eliminate biases, noting that while shuffling can be resource-intensive, it may only need to be done once before multiple data iterations.

children.title: Importance of Shuffling Data in Sampling
children.summary: Shuffling data is crucial in sampling to eliminate biases that can affect results, especially in stochastic gradient descent. The speaker discusses the practice of shuffling data, noting that while it can be expensive, it may only need to be done once before walking through the data multiple times. This approach helps avoid issues where similar data points are grouped together, which can lead to ineffective learning cycles.

If all the things that are the same or beside each other and they cause me to just run backwards and then all the other things that are the same cause we run forwards, I just get stuck in this kind of forward backwards loop. children.title: Stochastic Brain Descent and Feature Engineering
children.summary: The theory of stochastic brain descent suggests that a single random sample can yield unbiased estimates that converge to the optimal solution under certain loss conditions, with improved convergence speed as sample sizes increase. The discussion includes the computational demands for models with up to 400 billion dimensions and trillions of tokens, emphasizing the role of stochastic gradient descent (SGD) in the 2020s. A poll on gradient updates per epoch for 8192 datapoints with a batch size of 1 is also mentioned. Additionally, the importance of feature engineering in linear models, which form the foundation of complex neural networks, is highlighted, along with transformations like logarithmic or polynomial adjustments to achieve linearity and enhance optimization techniques.

children.title: Stochastic Brain Descent and Feature Engineering
children.summary: The theory of stochastic brain descent indicates that a single random sample can provide unbiased estimates that converge to the optimal solution under specific loss conditions, improving convergence speed with larger sample sizes. The segment discusses the computational requirements for models with up to 400 billion dimensions and trillions of tokens, highlighting the use of stochastic gradient descent (SGD) in the 2020s, including a poll on gradient updates per epoch for 8192 datapoints with a batch size of 1. It also stresses the significance of feature engineering in linear models, which serve as the basis for complex neural networks, and explores transformations such as logarithmic or polynomial adjustments to achieve linearity, thus enhancing optimization techniques.

children.title: Stochastic Brain Descent and Feature Engineering
children.summary: The theory of stochastic brain descent shows that using a single random sample can yield unbiased estimates that converge to the optimal solution under certain loss conditions, enhancing convergence speed with large sample sizes. The discussion includes the computational demands of models that can reach 400 billion dimensions and utilize trillions of tokens, focusing on the application of stochastic gradient descent (SGD) in the 2020s, including a poll on gradient updates per epoch for 8192 datapoints using SGD with a batch size of 1. Additionally, it emphasizes the importance of feature engineering in linear models, which are foundational for complex neural networks, and discusses transformations like logarithmic or polynomial adjustments to achieve linearity, facilitating effective optimization techniques.

children.title: Stochastic Brain Descent and Gradient Estimation
children.summary: The theory of stochastic brain descent utilizes a single random sample to demonstrate that unbiased estimates can lead to convergence towards the optimal solution under specific loss conditions. This method is advantageous as it allows for multiple steps with substantial estimates, enhancing convergence speed. By employing large sample sizes, the process approximates the analysis of all data, facilitating effective gradient descent techniques in practice.

children.title: Feature Engineering and Linear Models
children.summary: Linear models are foundational in modeling, including complex neural networks, which ultimately rely on linear relationships. Feature engineering is crucial for transforming data to fit these linear models, such as using logarithmic or polynomial transformations to achieve linearity. This allows for the application of optimization techniques on seemingly simple linear models, enhancing their effectiveness.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: You now know it. And we can find a new stuff. engineering. This used to be a couple lectures. We're going to boil us down to a few lectures. One lecture 30 minutes we can do it. The idea here are pretty simple. We use linear models for everything. In fact, even these big neural networks at the very top, it's just a linear model. And the art is making my data. Work for linear model. A lot of what model building? Is finding ways to make... My relationships linear. And that is what feature-engineing is in transforming my data. So it looks like this. What if I take the long of it? Now it's a linear model and maybe I take the fourth power of my wise transfer my The thing I'm trying to predict as well. So I transform my data so that it lives along a line. I can use all this really great machinery for optimizing my model. And it can be a very simple linear model. That's future engineering. children.title: Feature Engineering and One-Hot Encoding Techniques
children.summary: Feature engineering enhances model performance by transforming raw features and capturing non-linear relationships, including the use of transformations like squares and logs. One-hot encoding is a key technique for converting categorical features into binary formats suitable for regression, ensuring equal representation of categories. The segment discusses the impact of one-hot encoding on model complexity, demonstrating its use in linear regression with scikit-learn's LinearRegression class. It highlights how coefficients indicate the predictive power of features, such as days of the week affecting tips, and includes a practical example of predicting tips based on specific inputs. The discussion also addresses the implications of excluding intercepts in one-hot encoded models and the challenges related to matrix rank.

children.title: Feature Engineering and Transformations Overview
children.summary: Feature engineering enhances model performance by transforming raw features, incorporating domain knowledge, and capturing non-linear relationships in linear models. It includes applying feature functions that can increase dataset dimensionality, such as adding squared features to the design matrix. Transformations may involve nonlinear functions like squares, logs, and products of features. The importance of encoding categorical data for regression analysis is also emphasized.

children.title: Feature Engineering and Transformations Overview
children.summary: Feature engineering enhances model performance by transforming raw features, allowing for the incorporation of domain knowledge and capturing non-linear relationships in linear models. It involves applying feature functions that can increase dataset dimensionality, such as adding squared features to the design matrix. Transformations can include nonlinear functions like squares, logs, and products of features, and the importance of encoding categorical data for regression analysis is emphasized.

children.title: Feature Engineering and Transforming Variables
children.summary: Feature engineering involves transforming raw features to enhance model performance, particularly in linear models. It allows for the incorporation of domain knowledge to create new features that capture specific shapes or express non-linear relationships. The effectiveness of linear modeling is contingent on the presence of linear relationships in the dataset, and previous lectures demonstrated that applying transformations can help linearize datasets.

children.title: Feature Engineering and Functions Overview
children.summary: Feature engineering involves transforming raw features into more informative features for modeling, allowing the capture of domain knowledge, expression of non-linear relationships using linear models, and incorporation of non-numeric (qualitative) features. Feature functions describe the transformations applied to raw features, often increasing the dimensionality of the dataset. An example provided is a feature function that adds a squared feature to the design matrix, illustrating the transformation process with a dataset of raw features.

children.title: Transformations Using Feature Functions
children.summary: Feature functions transform raw features into new feature spaces, often increasing the dataset's dimensionality. An example includes adding squared features to the design matrix, such as hp and hp^2 for a dataset of car specifications. Linear models trained on these transformed features can be represented using the symbol @ instead of X, indicating the use of transformed variables in the model equations.

children.title: Feature Transformations and Functions
children.summary: Feature transformations involve applying various functions to raw data to create new features, which can expand the dimensionality of the dataset. Examples include nonlinear transformations such as squares, logs, and products of features. The notation for transformed features is introduced, and the importance of encoding categorical data, such as words, for regression analysis is highlighted. The segment also touches on the use of symbols in linear models trained on transformed data.

children.title: One-Hot Encoding for Categorical Features
children.summary: One-hot encoding is a feature engineering technique that transforms qualitative features into numeric formats suitable for regression models. It creates new binary columns for each category of a qualitative feature, such as days of the week or regions, where '1' indicates the presence and '0' indicates absence of that feature value. This method effectively represents categorical data, ensuring equal representation of all categories without inherent ordering, thus enhancing the dataset beyond numeric features.

children.title: One-Hot Encoding for Categorical Features
children.summary: One-hot encoding is a feature engineering technique used to convert qualitative (non-numeric) features into numeric formats suitable for regression models. It involves creating new binary columns for each category of a qualitative feature, such as days of the week or regions, where a '1' indicates the presence of that feature value and '0' indicates absence. This method allows for effective representation of categorical data, ensuring that all categories are equally represented without any inherent ordering, thus expanding the dataset beyond just numeric features.

children.title: One-hot Encoding for Non-Numeric Features
children.summary: One-hot encoding is introduced as a feature engineering technique to incorporate qualitative (non-numeric) features into regression models. The process involves creating new binary columns for each category of a qualitative feature, such as the day of the week. For example, a 'region' column can be transformed into multiple columns, each representing a different device, where a '1' indicates the presence of that feature value and '0' indicates absence. This method allows the inclusion of qualitative features like day, color, or size in regression models, expanding the dataset beyond just numeric features.

children.title: One-hot Encoding in Feature Engineering
children.summary: One-hot encoding is a technique used in feature engineering to convert qualitative data into numeric features suitable for modeling. Each category of a categorical variable is assigned its own feature, where the value is set to 1 if a row belongs to that category and 0 otherwise. This process allows for the effective representation of categorical data in a numerical format, facilitating its use in various modeling algorithms.

children.title: One-Hot Encoding in Feature Engineering
children.summary: One-hot encoding is a technique used to convert qualitative data into numeric features for modeling. Each category of a categorical variable is assigned its own feature, where the value is set to 1 if a row belongs to that category and 0 otherwise. This method ensures that no feature is prioritized over another, maintaining an equal representation of nominal data without any inherent ordering.

children.title: One-Hot Encoding for Categorical Data
children.summary: One-hot encoding is a feature engineering technique that transforms qualitative data into numeric features for modeling. Each category of a categorical variable is represented by its own feature, with a value of 1 indicating that a row belongs to that category and 0 otherwise. This method allows for the encoding of qualitative features, such as days of the week, into a format suitable for linear models. The one-hot encoded features can then be utilized in the design matrix to train a model effectively.

children.title: One-Hot Encoding for Regression Analysis
children.summary: The segment discusses the importance of encoding categorical variables, specifically the day of the week, for regression analysis. It introduces one-hot encoding as a method to convert string data into numerical format suitable for regression. An example is provided with a dataset showing total bill amounts and sizes, demonstrating how to create one-hot encoded features for different days. The regression equation is presented in shorthand, illustrating how the encoded features can be integrated into the design matrix for model training.

def sgd(grad, X, y, initial_theta, eta = 0.3, max_iter = 5000, batch_size=50 ):
theta = initial_theta
thetas = [theta]
n = len (X)

X_sample = X.sample(batch_size)
y_sample = y. loc [X_sample. index]
theta = theta - eta/t * grad(theta, X_sample, y_sample)
thetas. append (theta)
return thetas

It turns out that the examples we looked at above represent a somewhat artificial scenario: we trained our model on all the data we had available, then used the model to make pre Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
dataset. A more realistic situation is when we wish to apply our model on unseen data - that is, datapoints that it did not encounter during the model fitting process.
Suppose we obtain a random sample of 6 datapoints from our population of vehicle data. We want to train a model on these 6 points and use it to make predictions on unseen data (perhaps cars for which
we don't already know the true mpg ).
[*]:
np. random. seed (100)
sample_6 = vehicles.sample(6)
sns. scatterplot(data=sample_6, x="hp", y="mpg")
plt.ylim(-35, 50)
plt.xlim(0, 250);
If we design a model with polynomial degree 5, we can make perfect predictions on this sample of training data.
[*]:
degree_5_model = Pipeline( [
('polynomial_transformation', PolynomialFeatures(5)),
('linear_regression', LinearRegression( ) )
degree_5_model. fit(sample_6[["hp"] ], sample_6 ["mpg"] )
xs = np. linspace(0, 250, 1000)
degree_5_model_predictions = degree_5_model.predict(xs[ :, np.newaxis] )
plt.plot(xs, degree_5_model_predictions, c="tab: red")
sns. scatterplot(data=sample_6, x="hp", y="mpg", s=50)
plt.ylim(-35, 50)
plt.xlim(0, 250);
However, when we reapply this fitted model to the full population of data, it fails to capture the major trends of the dataset.
[*] :
plt.plot(xs, degree_5_model_predictions, c="tab: red")
sns. scatterplot(data=vehicles, x="hp", y="mpg", s=50)
plt.ylim(-35, 50);
The model has overfit to the data used to train it. It has essentially "memorized" the six datapoints used during model fitting, and does not generalize well to new data.

Because day is non-numeric, we will apply one-hot encoding before fitting a model.
The OneHotEncoder class of sklearn (documentation) offers a quick way to perform one-hot encoding. You will explore its use in detail in Lab 7. For now, recognize that we follow a very similar
workflow to when we were working with the LinearRegression class: we initialize a OneHotEncoder object, fit it to our data, then use . transform to apply the fitted encoder.
[4]:

It turns out that the examples we looked at above represent a somewhat artificial scenario: we trained our model on all the data we had available, then used the model to make pre Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
dataset. A more realistic situation is when we wish to apply our model on unseen data - that is, datapoints that it did not encounter during the model fitting process.
Suppose we obtain a random sample of 6 datapoints from our population of vehicle data. We want to train a model on these 6 points and use it to make predictions on unseen data (perhaps cars for which
we don't already know the true mpg ).
[*]:
np. random. seed (100)
sample_6 = vehicles.sample(6)
sns. scatterplot(data=sample_6, x="hp", y="mpg")
plt.ylim(-35, 50)
plt.xlim(0, 250);
If we design a model with polynomial degree 5, we can make perfect predictions on this sample of training data.
[*]:
degree_5_model = Pipeline( [
('polynomial_transformation', PolynomialFeatures(5)),
('linear_regression', LinearRegression( ) )
degree_5_model. fit(sample_6[["hp"] ], sample_6 ["mpg"] )
xs = np. linspace(0, 250, 1000)
degree_5_model_predictions = degree_5_model.predict(xs[ :, np.newaxis] )
plt.plot(xs, degree_5_model_predictions, c="tab: red")
sns. scatterplot(data=sample_6, x="hp", y="mpg", s=50)
plt.ylim(-35, 50)
plt.xlim(0, 250);
However, when we reapply this fitted model to the full population of data, it fails to capture the major trends of the dataset.
[*] :
plt.plot(xs, degree_5_model_predictions, c="tab: red")
sns. scatterplot(data=vehicles, x="hp", y="mpg", s=50)
plt.ylim(-35, 50);
The model has overfit to the data used to train it. It has essentially "memorized" the six datapoints used during model fitting, and does not generalize well to new data.

def sgd(grad, X, y, initial_theta, eta = 0.3, max_iter = 5000, batch_size=50 ):

theta = theta - eta/t * grad(theta, X_sample, y_sample)
thetas. append (theta)
return thetas
