It turns out that the examples we looked at above represent a somewhat artificial scenario: we trained our model on all the data we had available, then used the model to make pre Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
dataset. A more realistic situation is when we wish to apply our model on unseen data - that is, datapoints that it did not encounter during the model fitting process.
Suppose we obtain a random sample of 6 datapoints from our population of vehicle data. We want to train a model on these 6 points and use it to make predictions on unseen data (perhaps cars for which
we don't already know the true mpg ).
[*]:
np. random. seed (100)
sample_6 = vehicles.sample(6)
sns. scatterplot(data=sample_6, x="hp", y="mpg")
plt.ylim(-35, 50)
plt.xlim(0, 250);
If we design a model with polynomial degree 5, we can make perfect predictions on this sample of training data.
[*]:
degree_5_model = Pipeline( [
('polynomial_transformation', PolynomialFeatures(5)),
('linear_regression', LinearRegression( ) )
degree_5_model. fit(sample_6[["hp"] ], sample_6 ["mpg"] )
xs = np. linspace(0, 250, 1000)
degree_5_model_predictions = degree_5_model.predict(xs[ :, np.newaxis] )
plt.plot(xs, degree_5_model_predictions, c="tab: red")
sns. scatterplot(data=sample_6, x="hp", y="mpg", s=50)
plt.ylim(-35, 50)
plt.xlim(0, 250);
However, when we reapply this fitted model to the full population of data, it fails to capture the major trends of the dataset.
[*] :
plt.plot(xs, degree_5_model_predictions, c="tab: red")
sns. scatterplot(data=vehicles, x="hp", y="mpg", s=50)
plt.ylim(-35, 50);
The model has overfit to the data used to train it. It has essentially "memorized" the six datapoints used during model fitting, and does not generalize well to new data.

children.title: One-Hot Encoding with Scikit-Learn
children.summary: One-hot encoding is introduced as a method for processing categorical data, specifically for the 'day' feature which is non-numeric. The OneHotEncoder class from scikit-learn is highlighted, detailing the workflow of initializing the encoder, fitting it to the data, and using the transform method to convert categorical values into a numerical format. An example code snippet demonstrates how to initialize and fit the OneHotEncoder to the 'day' feature in a dataset.

workflow to when we were working with the LinearRegression class: we initialize a OneHotEncoder object, fit it to our data, then use . transform to apply the fitted encoder.

children.title: Handling Invalid Values in One-Hot Encoding
children.summary: Discusses the issue of encountering invalid values, such as 'FUNDAY', when using one-hot encoding for categorical data. It emphasizes the importance of ensuring that all potential values are included in the training data to avoid prediction errors when new, unseen categories appear. The segment also includes a practical example of fitting a OneHotEncoder to a dataset and transforming the 'day' feature, demonstrating how to handle valid and invalid inputs.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What happens if I say? Fun day! I want to hang on to this. What's your happen? What's the one-hot and quitting a fun day? Air, why? Fun day is not a day of the week that it's all. So if I have a, a fun day column or a fun day value in that column, I should drop it. Why might this happen I might have trained on a perfectly reasonable days of the week get a set I'm going to test this. On some new customer who comes in says I'm here on FUNDAY. And as you get an error because I don't know how to include fun day. It's not unique and not a valid value. Some more important if I were trying to let's say model majors and I have like all the majors that I've seen in my training data and then Berkeley creates new major and I try to make prediction. I don't have that major when I fit the data I can't make a prediction for it. Video: 1: File Edit View Run Kernel Tabs Settings Help

children.title: Data Transformation, One-Hot Encoding, and Model Complexity
children.summary: The segment discusses data transformation and feature engineering for linear regression, focusing on one-hot encoding to handle categorical variables. It explains how coefficients reflect the impact of different days on tips, noting that Fridays and Sundays are more predictive. The use of scikit-learn's LinearRegression class is demonstrated, fitting models without a bias column and presenting coefficients for features like total_bill and size. The importance of one-hot encoding in enhancing model expressiveness and simplifying debugging is emphasized, with a practical example predicting tips for a party of size 3 with a $50 bill on a Friday. The discussion also covers the exclusion of intercepts in one-hot encoded models, the implications for matrix rank, and the challenges of inverting rank 3 matrices. Regularization techniques are introduced to address issues with bias terms and ensure valid ordinary least squares estimates. Finally, the segment touches on increasing model complexity with polynomial features.

children.title: Data Transformation and Feature Engineering in Linear Regression
children.summary: The segment covers data transformation for linear regression, emphasizing the handling of categorical variables through one-hot encoding to convert them into a numerical format. It highlights that coefficients from the linear model reflect the impact of different days of the week on tips, noting that Fridays and Sundays have higher predictive power. The scikit-learn library's LinearRegression class is used to fit models without a bias column, presenting coefficients for features like total_bill, size, and day of the week. Additionally, the importance of one-hot encoding in enhancing model expressiveness and simplifying debugging is discussed, including a practical example predicting tips for a party of size 3 with a $50 bill on a Friday. The segment concludes with an overview of code implementation using sklearn, featuring ColumnTransformer and PolynomialFeatures for effective feature engineering.

children.title: Data Transformation for Linear Regression
children.summary: The segment discusses the process of transforming data for linear regression analysis, specifically focusing on handling categorical variables. It mentions the use of one-hot encoding to convert categorical data into a numerical format suitable for regression models. The coefficients obtained from the linear model indicate the impact of different days of the week on the resulting tips.

The OneHotEncoder has converted the categorical day feature into four numeric features! Recall that the tips dataset only
included data for Thursday through Sunday, which is why only four days of the week appear.

matrix only includes numeric values.

children.title: Feature Engineering and Model Fitting with Scikit Learn
children.summary: Fridays and Sundays show higher positive predictive power for tips. The scikit-learn library's LinearRegression class is utilized to fit a model to the design matrix without adding a bias column. The model coefficients for features such as total_bill, size, and day of the week are presented. Additionally, the scikit-learn Pipeline class allows for the creation of sophisticated feature engineering pipelines that combine various transformations and models, enabling simultaneous fitting of the entire pipeline.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What might I conclude here? Fridays and Sundays seem to have higher positive predictive power have higher effect on my tip. Good days to tip. Alright, bonus features not only, you know, future final. A psychic learn makes it easy to actually compose various feature or transformations into one giant pipeline that you can fit the entire pipeline all at once and then say predict and it does all of this stuff for you. And it manages it keeps track of it, draws cute pictures of everything that's happening. Video: 1: File Edit View Run Kernel Tabs Settings Help

A pipeline is a sequence of transformations and models that are composed to form a new model. It is implemented as a list of ("name",
model/transformation) tuples. Here we have a feature engineering stage followed by a linear model. The feature engineering stage uses a
ColumnTransformer that applies named transformations to selected columns.
[10]:
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn. compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.preprocessing import PolynomialFeatures
pl = Pipeline ( [
("Feature Engineering",
ColumnTransformer(
[("OHE", OneHotEncoder(), ["day"] ),
("Polynomial", PolynomialFeatures(4), ['total_bill' ] )],
remainder="passthrough",
force_int_remainder_cols=False)),
("Linear Model", LinearRegression( )) # Linear Model
pl

children.title: One-Hot Encoding and Model Predictions
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the importance of one-hot encoding in modeling, particularly how it enhances model expressiveness and simplifies debugging. A practical example is presented where the predicted tip for a party of size 3 with a $50 bill on a Friday is analyzed. The significance of the one-hot encoded feature for the day of the week is emphasized, demonstrating how it influences predictions based on its coefficient. The segment concludes with a brief overview of a code implementation using sklearn, showcasing the use of ColumnTransformer and PolynomialFeatures for feature engineering.

children.title: One-Hot Encoding: Intercepts, Matrix Rank, and Regularization
children.summary: The discussion covers the exclusion of intercepts in one-hot encoded models, explaining that each row has one active feature, leading to consistent sums. A worked example illustrates predicting tips based on total bill, party size, and day of the week, detailing feature coefficients and interpretations. It further explains adding an intercept term via a column of ones and its impact on matrix rank, noting that even with four columns, the rank remains three due to linear combinations. The challenges of inverting a rank 3 matrix are discussed, emphasizing that the bias term is redundant in one-hot encoding. Regularization techniques are highlighted to mitigate issues with bias terms, stressing the importance of omitting one-hot encoded columns to prevent linear dependence and ensure valid ordinary least squares estimates.

children.title: Exclusion of Intercept in One-Hot Models
children.summary: The discussion focuses on why an intercept term is not included in one-hot encoded models. It explores the implications of summing one-hot features across rows, noting that if each row has exactly one active feature, the sum will yield a consistent value. The segment also includes a worked example involving a model predicting tips based on total bill, party size, and day of the week, illustrating the coefficients for each feature and their interpretations.

children.title: Matrix Rank and One-Hot Encoding Explanation
children.summary: The segment discusses the addition of an intercept term in a matrix through a column of all ones, which serves as the bias term. It explores the implications for the matrix's rank, noting that despite having four columns, the rank is only three because three of the columns can be combined to equal the fourth. Additionally, it emphasizes that any set of one-hot encoded columns will sum to a column of all ones, reinforcing the concept of linear combinations in matrix rank.

children.title: Matrix Inversion and One-Hot Encoding
children.summary: The discussion centers on the challenge of inverting a matrix when its rank is 3. The speaker mentions the process of taking the transpose of x and inverting it, noting that typically, a bias term would not be added since it is already a linear combination of existing one-hot encoded columns. Furthermore, it is emphasized that any set of one-hot encoded columns will sum to a column of all ones, illustrating this with an example of original data for days of the week and the corresponding one-hot encoding.

children.title: Regularization and One-Hot Encoding in Models
children.summary: Regularization techniques can help address issues with bias terms in models. When using one-hot encoding (OHE), it's important to avoid including the bias term, as it can lead to linear dependence in the model, making it non-invertible and causing issues with ordinary least squares (OLS) estimates. To resolve this, one can omit one of the one-hot encoded columns or not include an intercept term. Including a bias column creates linear combinations with OHE columns, which is undesirable. New columns should not be linear combinations of existing ones to enhance model performance.

children.title: Polynomial Features and One-Hot Encoding
children.summary: Polynomial features can be added to a linear model to improve fit by transforming the data, such as squaring variables like horsepower. This maintains the linearity of the model in terms of parameters, allowing for non-linear relationships within the features. Adding more polynomial terms can lead to better fitting of the data, but there is a caution about overfitting. Additionally, one-hot encoding is discussed, emphasizing that omitting one of the encoded columns retains the same information as the remaining columns, since the omitted column is a linear combination of the others.

children.title: Model Complexity and Error Trends with Polynomial Features
children.summary: Increasing model complexity by adding polynomial features decreases training error, as shown by decreasing Mean Squared Error (MSE) values with higher polynomial degrees. The discussion emphasizes the relationship between model complexity and the concepts of underfitting and overfitting, illustrated through graphical representations of MSE changes with varying polynomial degrees. While higher complexity reduces training error, it also risks overfitting if not managed appropriately.

children.title: Model Complexity and Polynomial Features
children.summary: Increasing model complexity by adding polynomial features leads to a decrease in training error, as evidenced by the decreasing Mean Squared Error (MSE) values with higher polynomial degrees. The discussion highlights the general trend of underfitting and overfitting in relation to model complexity, illustrating how training MSE changes as the degree of the polynomial model increases.

children.title: Model Complexity and Error Trends
children.summary: As model complexity increases, the training error decreases, which is illustrated through the addition of polynomial features. The presentation discusses the general trend observed in datasets, highlighting the concepts of underfitting and overfitting. A graphical representation shows how Mean Squared Error (MSE) changes with varying degrees of polynomial complexity, emphasizing that while increasing complexity may reduce training error, it can lead to overfitting if not managed properly.

children.title: Polynomial Fitting and Model Performance
children.summary: A polynomial of degree N-1 can perfectly fit N non-overlapping data points, resulting in zero error, exemplified by a degree-4 polynomial fitting five points. However, when applied to real data, such as a training dataset of six points, the fit may not perform well, illustrating the discrepancy between perfect fits on training data and actual performance on unseen data.

children.title: Polynomial Fitting and Model Performance
children.summary: A polynomial of degree N-1 can perfectly fit N non-overlapping data points, resulting in zero error. For example, a degree-4 polynomial can model a dataset of five points accurately. However, when applying this to real data, the fit may not perform well, as demonstrated by a new example with a training dataset of six points, highlighting the difference between perfect fits on training data and actual performance on unseen data.

children.title: Model Complexity, Generalization, and Bias-Variance Tradeoff
children.summary: The segment discusses the significance of model complexity and generalization, using an example of a training dataset with six datapoints. It illustrates the temptation to create a complex model, such as a degree 5 polynomial, which perfectly fits the training data but leads to poor performance on unseen data, highlighting the risk of overfitting. The variance trade-off is introduced, noting that complex models increase prediction variability across different samples. The concept of the bias-variance tradeoff is mentioned, with a focus on the importance of feature engineering for an upcoming assignment. Future lectures will cover tools for managing this tradeoff, and a reminder is given about the next lecture on Thursday.

children.title: Model Complexity and Generalization Issues
children.summary: The discussion highlights the importance of building models that accurately represent the data while acknowledging that available data may not capture the complete picture of the world. A new example is introduced involving a training dataset of only six datapoints, illustrating the temptation to create a highly complex model (e.g., degree 5 polynomial) that fits the training data perfectly. However, this complexity leads to poor performance on unseen data, emphasizing the risk of overfitting and the need for models that generalize well to new populations.

children.title: Generalization, Overfitting, and Model Variance
children.summary: The concept of generalization in modeling is introduced, highlighting the variance trade-off where more complex models can lead to increased variability in predictions based on different samples. An example is provided with a training dataset of 6 points, illustrating how a complex degree-5 model perfectly predicts the training data but fails to generalize to unseen data, resulting in overfitting. The sensitivity of complex models to specific training datasets is discussed, emphasizing that they exhibit high variance and can produce erratic predictions when trained on different samples.

Audio: And this... Idea is generalization. And in a future lecture we'll talk about this variance trade-off that as I get. So as I make my models more crazy, as I add more polynomial and invent new features, it is likely my variance increases. And this puts me in this world I'm over-fitting. children.title: Bias-Variance Tradeoff and Feature Engineering
children.summary: The discussion introduces the bias-variance tradeoff, which will be explored in a future lecture. Emphasis is placed on the importance of feature engineering in the upcoming assignment, where students will create new features and fit their data. Tools for managing the bias-variance tradeoff will also be covered in future sessions. The segment concludes with a reminder of the next lecture on Thursday.
